1: <header>Environmental and economic analysis of power generation in a thermophilic biogas plant </header>
D. Ruiz G. San Miguel a,* , B. Corona a A. Gaitero A. Domínguez C

<list>a Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Industrial Engineering Faculty (ETSII), Universidad Politécnica of Madrid, José Gutiérrez Abascal 2, 28006 Madrid, Spain 
b Biogas Fuel Cell S.A., Parque Científico Tecnológico, Ada Byron 107, 33203 Gijón, Spain

2:
<list>Life cycle assessment, life cycle costing and cost benefit analysis were performed. 
Operation and maintenance of the biogas plant are the most impacting activities. 
Anaerobic digestion of biowaste results in negative values for climate change. 
Income obtained from waste treatment is the main input to the plant profitability.</list>

4: The inadequate management of residues may cause serious impacts on the environment, such as water and soil contamination, air pollution and human health problems. However, wastes may also be seen as a re- source for the production of new products or the generation of energy,

thus contributing to the conservation of natural resources and to sus- tainable development. Anaerobic digestion is a mature technology widely used to transform fermentable biomass residues into a biogas consisting primarily of methane and carbon dioxide. This biogas may then be used directly for its heating value, it may be converted to elec- tricity and heat through cogeneration (CHP) or it may be upgraded to biomethane for use as a replacement to natural gas (Da Costa Gomez, 2013; Minguela and Godoy, 2015). Hence, biogas plants fulfill two func- tions: first, the management of residues; and second the generation of

energy. The potential of biogas is acknowledged and amply covered by the International Energy Agency in Task 37: Energy from Biogas (Bioenergy, 2008). In the European Union, 13.4 X 106 tons oil equiva- lent (Mtoe) of biogas and 52.3 X 106 MWh of biogas electricity were produced in 2013 (Eurobserv-ER, 2014). This represents a 10.2% growth with respect to 2012 and 28.8% when compared with 2011.

Biogas technology is not unique in its design and scale, ranging from small domestic digesters, medium size plants utilizing farming or wastewater treatment residues, to large centralized co-digestion instal- lations (Deublein and Steinhauser, 2011). These plants typically operate using locally available fermentable feedstocks in order to minimize transportation costs (Deublein and Steinhauser, 2011; Minguela and Godoy, 2015). The most common biogas substrates include animal slurry, sewage sludge, farming and food-processing waste and domestic refuse. The use of energy crops (including maize, crucifers, grasses, etc.) is also becoming widespread in certain regions of Germany, Italy and France benefiting both from adequate climatic/soil conditions and fa- vorable regulatory frameworks that promote the generation of renew- able energies (Eurobserv-ER, 2014; Gissén et al., 2014).

Despite its renewable nature and green perception, the production and utilization of biogas generates environmental impacts that need to be adequately identified and quantified so that they may be managed accordingly. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is a well-established method- ology widely used to assess the environmental sustainability of energy systems (Cherubini et al., 2009; San Miguel et al., 2015). Various publi- cations have been dedicated to analyze the environmental performance of biogas systems in different locations, using different substrates and based on installations of different characteristics (Ishikawa et al., 2006; Cherubini et al., 2009; Jury et al., 2010; Adelt et al., 2011; Stucki et al., 2011; Poeschl et al., 2012a, 2012b; Rehl et al., 2012; Hahn et al., 2015; Xu al., 2015; Hijazi et al., 2016). These investigations show sig- nificant methodological divergences regarding the definition of the Function and its Functional Unit (processed substrate mass, biogas vol- ume, biomethane volume, power generation, heat generation), the allo- cation of impacts to different functions, the system boundaries (cradle to gate VS. cradle to grave), the selection of impact categories and the impact assessment methodology (end point VS. midpoint). The results reported suggest that the environmental performance of biogas systems are affected primarily by the type of feedstock, with significantly higher impacts associated with the use of energy crops (Adelt et al., 2011; Hahn et al., 2015; Jury et al., 2010; Poeschl et al., 2012a, 2012b) and lower for substrates of a residual nature (Ishikawa et al., 2006; Xu et al., 2015). Emissions associated with the storage of substrates and digestates have been reported to have a notable effect primarily on the climate change, toxicity and acidification categories, thus being recommended the use of devices to collect diffuse biogas emissions and the installation of gas flares. All these impacts may also be reduced by improving energy efficiency in the biogas plant and in the auxiliary elements (CHP, boiler, biogas upgrading, etc.) and also by considering the use of digestate as fertilizers (Hahn et al., 2015; Hijazi et al., 2016).

Evaluating sustainability requires going beyond the conventional environmental LCA and incorporating into the analysis economic and social performance indicators (UNEP, 2011). Life cycle costing (LCC) is a management tool employed to quantify the monetary costs associated with the life cycle of a product or service (Dhillon, 2009; Hunkeler et al., 2008). This inventory data includes all the costs borne by the promoter throughout the life cycle of the project (including capital, operation and end-of-life expenses) and also revenues derived from the selling of products or provision of services. In a biogas system, these may include the marketing of heat, electricity, provision of waste management ser- vices and also the commercialization of digestate as fertilizer. In order to take into consideration time effects, cash flows generated throughout the lifetime of the project need to be discounted to present values. LCC analysis may be expanded in complexity to take into consideration ex- ternal costs (EXTERNE, 2017) or it may be supplemented with a Cost Benefit Analysis (CBA) with information about the financial

attractiveness of the project. The integration of these environmental and economic evaluation tools, using the same scope and assumptions, provides a more comprehensive insight into the sustainability of the biogas system, thus enabling decision makers to consider both criteria in a coherent manner.

The objective of this paper is to produce a holistic evaluation of the environmental and economic performance of a commercial biogas plant using a life cycle approach. This has involved integrating LCA, LCC and CBA methodologies under the same scope. The analysis is based on economic and technical data from a state of the art commercial installation located in northern Spain and considers two alternative sce- narios regarding the commercial use of digestate as a replacement for synthetic fertilizers.

The use of energy crops (including maize, crucifers, grasses, etc.) is also becoming widespread in certain regions of Germany, Italy and France benefiting both from adequate climatic/soil conditions and fa- vorable regulatory frameworks that promote the generation of renew- able energies (Eurobserv-ER, 2014; Gissén et al., 2014).

Despite its renewable nature and green perception, the production and utilization of biogas generates environmental impacts that need to be adequately identified and quantified so that they may be managed accordingly. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is a well-established method- ology widely used to assess the environmental sustainability of energy systems (Cherubini et al., 2009; San Miguel et al., 2015). Various publi- cations have been dedicated to analyze the environmental performance of biogas systems in different locations, using different substrates and based on installations of different characteristics (Ishikawa et al., 2006; Cherubini et al., 2009; Jury et al., 2010; Adelt et al., 2011; Stucki et al., 2011; Poeschl et al., 2012a, 2012b; Rehl et al., 2012; Hahn et al., 2015; Xu al., 2015; Hijazi et al., 2016). These investigations show sig- nificant methodological divergences regarding the definition of the Function and its Functional Unit (processed substrate mass, biogas vol- ume, biomethane volume, power generation, heat generation), the allo- cation of impacts to different functions, the system boundaries (cradle to gate VS. cradle to grave), the selection of impact categories and the impact assessment methodology (end point VS. midpoint). The results reported suggest that the environmental performance of biogas systems are affected primarily by the type of feedstock, with significantly higher impacts associated with the use of energy crops (Adelt et al., 2011; Hahn et al., 2015; Jury et al., 2010; Poeschl et al., 2012a, 2012b) and lower for substrates of a residual nature (Ishikawa et al., 2006; Xu et al., 2015). Emissions associated with the storage of substrates and digestates have been reported to have a notable effect primarily on the climate change, toxicity and acidification categories, thus being recommended the use of devices to collect diffuse biogas emissions and the installation of gas flares. All these impacts may also be reduced by improving energy efficiency in the biogas plant and in the auxiliary elements (CHP, boiler, biogas upgrading, etc.) and also by considering the use of digestate as fertilizers (Hahn et al., 2015; Hijazi et al., 2016).

Evaluating sustainability requires going beyond the conventional environmental LCA and incorporating into the analysis economic and social performance indicators (UNEP, 2011). Life cycle costing (LCC) is a management tool employed to quantify the monetary costs associated with the life cycle of a product or service (Dhillon, 2009; Hunkeler et al., 2008). This inventory data includes all the costs borne by the promoter throughout the life cycle of the project (including capital, operation and end-of-life expenses) and also revenues derived from the selling of products or provision of services. In a biogas system, these may include the marketing of heat, electricity, provision of waste management ser- vices and also the commercialization of digestate as fertilizer. In order to take into consideration time effects, cash flows generated throughout the lifetime of the project need to be discounted to present values. LCC analysis may be expanded in complexity to take into consideration ex- ternal costs (EXTERNE, 2017) or it may be supplemented with a Cost Benefit Analysis (CBA) with information about the financial

attractiveness of the project. The integration of these environmental and economic evaluation tools, using the same scope and assumptions, provides a more comprehensive insight into the sustainability of the biogas system, thus enabling decision makers to consider both criteria in a coherent manner.

The objective of this paper is to produce a holistic evaluation of the environmental and economic performance of a commercial biogas plant using a life cycle approach. This has involved integrating LCA, LCC and CBA methodologies under the same scope. The analysis is based on economic and technical data from a state of the art commercial installation located in northern Spain and considers two alternative sce- narios regarding the commercial use of digestate as a replacement for synthetic fertilizers.
9: <header>2.1. Goal and scope of the environmental and economic analyses </header>

The investigation is based on a newly built biogas plant designed and constructed in Tineo (Asturias, Spain) by the Spanish engineering com- pany Biogas Cell Fuel S.L. The installation is allowed to treat a wide range of biowaste materials using a state of the art multi-stage digestion pro- cess. Fig. 1 illustrates the different elements considered in the life cycle of the biogas system. The biowastes are transported from the point of origin to the biogas plant using tractors and trucks. Once delivered at the plant, the residues, received in liquid or semi-liquid form, are discharged into reception tanks where they are stored for no more than 48 h to avoid organic degradation and minimize greenhouse gas (GHG) and odour emissions. Reception tanks are connected to an indus- trial crusher where the residues are ground, mixed and homogenized prior to pasteurization at 70 °C for 1 h. The composition of the raw sub- strates and the mixing ratios are carefully controlled (in terms of pH, C/ N ratio, % volatile solids, etc.) in order to optimize the anaerobic diges- tion stage.

As shown in Table 1, the digestion phase consists of two parallel lines, each one including a primary 1000 thermophilic digester oper- ating at 55 followed by a secondary 975 m³ mesophilic digester oper- ating at 39 °C. Both reactors are based on continuous flow stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) technology and operate at hydraulic retention times (HRT) of 30 days. The two-step configuration and the long HRT ensure an effective digestion of the volatile solids and maximize biogas output. Two double membrane flexible 400 m³ gasometers mounted on top of the mesophilic digesters are used to collect and store the resulting bio- gas, which is then compressed to reduce water content and subjected to biological desulphurization to reduce H2S. The biogas feeds a combined heat and power (CHP) unit of 38% net electric efficiency and 48% ther- mal efficiency. Power is sold directly to the grid assuming no additional losses. The thermal energy is used onsite to heat the pasteurization pro- cess and the thermophilic digester.

The plant also has a pellet boiler for start up operations, a 420 kW se- curity torch to burn excess methane and a bio-filter to reduce odour emissions. The digestate generated in the biogas plant is accumulated in three independent 2000 m³ tanks from which it is collected free of charge by farmers and spread in the fields as fertilizer using drag hose technology.

The analysis described in this work is based on the biogas plant pro- cessing 26,000 t/yr of biowastes as follows: 6500 t/yr of pig slurry, 6500 t/yr of cow slurry, 7200 t/yr of sewage sludge, and 5800 t/yr of agri-food waste (comprising animal by-products from slaughterhouse activity and also fruit and vegetable residues). The biogas plant gener- ates 2000 MWh/yr of power, assuming 8000 h/yr full load operation of the 250 kW CHP unit.

In practice, commercial biogas plants have two functions: i) power generation and ii) management of biowaste materials. In order to avoid partitioning and impact allocation inaccuracies in the life cycle analysis of multifunctional systems like this one, ISO 14044
(*) End-of-Life compensated between biogas pathway and conventional treatment

13: <header>Fig. 1. Life cycle phases of electricity generation in the biogas plant. </header>

(14044:2006) recommends the use of a system expansion approach. In application of this methodology, power generation has been assumed as the only function of the biogas system, expanding the system bound- aries to include additional functions related to the end-of-life manage- ment of the biowastes (see more details in Section 2.2.4). Hence, all the impacts associated with biogas production have been attributed solely to power production while avoided environmental impacts de- rived from the additional functions of the biogas system have been subtracted.

In the case of pig/cow manure, the conventional end-of-life manage- ment practice involves open tank storage and field spreading as fertil- izer. For sewage sludge, this involves aerobic stabilization prior to its use as a fertilizer. End of life management for agri-food wastes involves its use for animal feeding: fruit and vegetable wastes are used directly as animal fodder while the slaughterhouse residues are used to produce compound feeding stuff.
Both the Life Cycle Analysis (LCA) and the Life Cycle Cost (LCC) anal- ysis have been structured considering the following life cycle phases:

<list>a) biowaste transport from the point of origin to the biogas plant, 
b) operation & maintenance, c) biogas plant and d) avoided impacts.</list>

15: <header>2.2. Life cycle inventory of the environmental analysis </header>

The following sections describe the life cycle inventory of the biogas system described as base case scenario. An alternative scenario consider- ing the use of digestate as a fertilizer is also described. This inventory data is based on real design and operating conditions, which were sup- plied directly from the biogas plant engineers.
16: <header>2.2.1. Waste transport </header>

As explained above, the biogas plant accepts different types of bio- logical residues. Burden allocation for the biogas substrates was made according to the Polluter Pays Principle which assumes no environmental burdens for waste materials.

Table 2 shows the biowaste inputs and the assumptions employed to simulate the environmental burdens associated with transporting the different wastes to the biogas plant. Activity data was calculated taking into consideration the loading capacity of the trucks (payload) and also the utilization ratios (workload), which were determined considering the transportation capacity of the cargos and the bulk density of the wastes. For the sewage sludge and the agri-food fractions, an empty re- turn of the trucks was assumed in the analyses. Tractors employed to transport the pig/cow manure were assumed to return with digestate. This activity data was applied to life cycle inventories from the European Life Cycle Database (ELCD 3.1), which include impacts attrib- utable to fuel production and combustion. Emissions due to tyre abra- sion, vehicle fabrication and maintenance, and construction of road
Table 2

infrastructures were also considered (Spielmann et al., 2007). Transport distances for each biogas substrate were calculated considering the lo- cation of the biowaste provider and also the mass supplied.
2.2.2. Operation and maintenance

This phase covers all the activities related to the operation and main- tenance (O&M) of the biogas plant including biowaste reception and biogas production. Table 3 describes in quantitative terms the main in- puts/outputs resulting from this life cycle phase, referenced to a func- tional unit (1 kWh). Activity data associated with the consumption of power, water and lubricants, as well as the generation of digestate and hazardous and no hazardous wastes, were quantified using a top/ down approach. This involved analyzing the internal accounting system of the biogas plant. Material and energy flows were divided by the aver- age biogas production of the plant in order to determine activity data per functional unit of product.

Power consumed onsite the biogas plant was assumed to be ob- tained from the grid. The electricity mix for purchased power was that of Spain in 2014 (REE, 2014). CH4 and N20 emissions from stored digestate in open tanks have been calculated considering the guidelines produced by the JRC (Giuntoli et al., 2014). Emission of unburnt meth- ane in biogas fueled engines, known as methane slip, is included in the analysis assuming the conventional values reported by the JRC (Giuntoli et al., 2014).

Impacts associated with land occupation of the biogas plant (0.8 have been shared by all the biogas produced throughout its lifetime. Emission factors for water, electricity and lubricant consumed in the biogas plant were obtained from Ecoinvent v3.1 database.
2.2.3. The biogas plant

This life cycle phase includes the raw materials and activities associ- ated with the construction and decommissioning of the biogas plant, its components and also the end of life (EoL) of all the materials employed in these two stages. Table 4 provides the inventory data associated with Construction and Dismantling (C&D) activities and Table 5 describes the inventories regarding the Equipment and Machinery (E&M) employed in the biogas plant. The environmental burdens calculated for each of the elements included in these two sections have been shared by all the biogas generated during the lifetime of the plant. Life cycle invento- ries for construction activities, materials and equipment have been ob- tained from Ecoinvent v3.1 datasets.

In particular, inventory data for the manufacturing of the cogenera- tion unit has been adapted from Ecoinvent v3.1 datasets specific for this type of equipment. Emissions for the manufacturing of items such as stirrers or pumps have been inventoried using a generic dataset for "heavy industrial machinery" using mass as reference. The number of units considered for each of the plant components has been calculated taking into account its life expectancy and that of the plant (20 years).
2.2.4. Avoided impacts

As stated above, in the absence of the biogas plant, the biowaste ma- terials would have followed conventional waste management routes leading to different functions and environmental burdens. A system ex- pansion approach was applied to include in the analysis the additional functions of the biogas system. Thus, all impacts attributable to biowastes transport, operation and maintenance, and construction and dismantling of the biogas plant have been allocated to the electricity

generated. Environmental burdens associated with the products ob- tained from the conventional management of these residues were cal- culated separately and subtracted from those determined for the generation of biogas power.

In order to quantify the environmental burdens associated with the conventional management routes of the biowastes, two impor- tant aspects were considered: A) environmental impacts arising from the conventional management of the biowastes; B) impact sav- ings of substituted services due to the fact that these biowastes are resources of interest for other activities. Therefore, it seems consis- tent that, for the biogas system expansion, substituting conventional management by the anaerobic digestion involves environmental savings from budget A, but the environmental attributions for bud- get B.

Emissions belonging to actions included in budget A include CH4 and N20 discharges caused by temporary storage of pig/cow manure in an uncovered tank before field application as fertilizer and also by transport of all biowaste materials used by conventional means. To determine these issues the following assumptions were made:

CH4 and N20 emissions caused by stored pig/cow manure were cal- culated according to the report produced by the Joint Research Cen- ter (Giuntoli et al., 2014). This report describes the assumptions made by the JRC when compiling default and typical GHG emissions for different solid and gaseous bioenergy pathways. The calculations made in this report are in accordance with the methodology set in COM (2010) (Commission, 2010a) and SWD (2014) 259 (Commission, 2014) in order to help economic operators to declare GHG emission savings associated with the production of electricity, heating and cooling from solid and gaseous biomass

sources. COM (2010) states sustainability criteria for solid and gas- eous bioenergy pathways which are similar to those reported in the Renewable Energy Directive (Commission, 2009a) and the Fuel Quality Directive (Commission, 2009b) for calculating the green- house impact of biofuels, bioliquids and their fossil fuels comparators.

The JRC report (Giuntoli et al., 2014) states that anaerobic digestion of manure should be considered as an improved agricultural manage- ment practice and that emissions derived from its storage must be con- sidered as a credit to the biogas pathway. This approach is in accordance with the system expansion proposed in this investigation by the authors.

Based on IPCC Guidelines, the ratio between methane emissions caused by the storage of manure and the storage of digestate is given by the reduction of volatile solids during digestion. This implies that, with the specific conditions assumed in this study (VS reduction = 43%), the credits methane emissions from the stored manure would be equal to 1/0.57 = 1.75 times the emissions from digestate storage. Considering that methane emission from the digestate are equal to 10.0% of the meth- ane produced at the biogas plant, the overall credits would be equal to 17.5% of the methane produced, i.e. 3.5 g CH4/MJ biogas.

Concerning N20 emissions, it has been considered that the propor- tion of ammoniacal nitrogen in the digestate is supposed to increase and that the total N is decreased due to losses in the digester, it is as- sumed that the net emissions from manure and digestate are equal and thus the credit would simply balance out the N20 emissions assigned to digestate storage. Thus, N20 avoided emission applied in this study was equal to 0.066 gN2O/MJ biogas.
17: <header>2.2.1. Waste transport </header>

As explained above, the biogas plant accepts different types of bio- logical residues. Burden allocation for the biogas substrates was made according to the Polluter Pays Principle which assumes no environmental burdens for waste materials.

Table 2 shows the biowaste inputs and the assumptions employed to simulate the environmental burdens associated with transporting the different wastes to the biogas plant. Activity data was calculated taking into consideration the loading capacity of the trucks (payload) and also the utilization ratios (workload), which were determined considering the transportation capacity of the cargos and the bulk density of the wastes. For the sewage sludge and the agri-food fractions, an empty re- turn of the trucks was assumed in the analyses. Tractors employed to transport the pig/cow manure were assumed to return with digestate. This activity data was applied to life cycle inventories from the European Life Cycle Database (ELCD 3.1), which include impacts attrib- utable to fuel production and combustion. Emissions due to tyre abra- sion, vehicle fabrication and maintenance, and construction of road
Table 2
Waste inputs and digestate outputs per functional unit of the biogas plant including type, origin and transportation requirements.

Feedstock|||Transport|Transport|Transport|Transport Origin||kg per 1 kWh|Vehicle|Round trip (km)|Load in departure (t)|Load in returnb (t) Pig manure|Livestock husbandry|3.25|Tractor|40|6|6 Cow manure||3.25|Tractor|40|6|6 Sewage sludge|Municipal waste water and from slaughterhouse|3.60|Truck, 22 t|60|14|0 Agri-food wastes|Fruit and vegetables, slaughterhouse and expired food|2.90|Truck, 40 t|120|20|0
a Weighted average from different origins. b Digestate output for farmers.

infrastructures were also considered (Spielmann et al., 2007). Transport distances for each biogas substrate were calculated considering the lo- cation of the biowaste provider and also the mass supplied.
2.2.2. Operation and maintenance

This phase covers all the activities related to the operation and main- tenance (O&M) of the biogas plant including biowaste reception and biogas production. Table 3 describes in quantitative terms the main in- puts/outputs resulting from this life cycle phase, referenced to a func- tional unit (1 kWh). Activity data associated with the consumption of power, water and lubricants, as well as the generation of digestate and hazardous and no hazardous wastes, were quantified using a top/ down approach. This involved analyzing the internal accounting system of the biogas plant. Material and energy flows were divided by the aver- age biogas production of the plant in order to determine activity data per functional unit of product.

Power consumed onsite the biogas plant was assumed to be ob- tained from the grid. The electricity mix for purchased power was that of Spain in 2014 (REE, 2014). CH4 and N20 emissions from stored digestate in open tanks have been calculated considering the guidelines produced by the JRC (Giuntoli et al., 2014). Emission of unburnt meth- ane in biogas fueled engines, known as methane slip, is included in the analysis assuming the conventional values reported by the JRC (Giuntoli et al., 2014).

Impacts associated with land occupation of the biogas plant (0.8 have been shared by all the biogas produced throughout its lifetime. Emission factors for water, electricity and lubricant consumed in the biogas plant were obtained from Ecoinvent v3.1 database.
2.2.3. The biogas plant

This life cycle phase includes the raw materials and activities associ- ated with the construction and decommissioning of the biogas plant, its components and also the end of life (EoL) of all the materials employed in these two stages. Table 4 provides the inventory data associated with Construction and Dismantling (C&D) activities and Table 5 describes the inventories regarding the Equipment and Machinery (E&M) employed in the biogas plant. The environmental burdens calculated for each of the elements included in these two sections have been shared by all the biogas generated during the lifetime of the plant. Life cycle invento- ries for construction activities, materials and equipment have been ob- tained from Ecoinvent v3.1 datasets.

In particular, inventory data for the manufacturing of the cogenera- tion unit has been adapted from Ecoinvent v3.1 datasets specific for this type of equipment. Emissions for the manufacturing of items such as stirrers or pumps have been inventoried using a generic dataset for "heavy industrial machinery" using mass as reference. The number of units considered for each of the plant components has been calculated taking into account its life expectancy and that of the plant (20 years).
2.2.4. Avoided impacts

As stated above, in the absence of the biogas plant, the biowaste ma- terials would have followed conventional waste management routes leading to different functions and environmental burdens. A system ex- pansion approach was applied to include in the analysis the additional functions of the biogas system. Thus, all impacts attributable to biowastes transport, operation and maintenance, and construction and dismantling of the biogas plant have been allocated to the electricity

Section|Product|Amount|EoL processes Plant construction|Concrete|2700 m³|Dismantling, sorting plant, 25% landfill and 75% as recycled aggregates Plant construction|Precast concrete|102,000 kg|Dismantling, sorting plant, 25% landfill and 75% as recycled aggregates Plant construction|Gravel and sand|1500 kg|Dismantling, sorting plant, 25% landfill and 75% as recycled aggregates Plant construction|Structural steel|67,000 kg|5% landfill and 95% recycling Plant construction|Bitumen|1000 kg|50% incineration, energy valorization and 50% landfill Plant construction|Stone|8500 kg|Dismantling, sorting plant, 100% landfill Plant construction|Excavation|14,000 m³| Plant construction|Machinery|650 h| Plant construction|Land transformation, from agriculture|8000 m²| Electric installation and piping|Electric installation (wiring)|430 kg|2% landfill and 98% recycling Electric installation and piping|HDPE piping|2400 kg|20% landfill and 80% recycling Electric installation and piping|PVC piping|3400 kg|50% incineration, energy valorization and 50% landfill Electric installation and piping|Copper piping|453 kg|5% landfill and 95% recycling Electric installation and piping|Stainless steel piping|4350 kg|5% landfill and 95% recycling Electric installation and piping|Steel Piping|11,300 kg|5% landfill and 95% recycling

generated. Environmental burdens associated with the products ob- tained from the conventional management of these residues were cal- culated separately and subtracted from those determined for the generation of biogas power.

In order to quantify the environmental burdens associated with the conventional management routes of the biowastes, two impor- tant aspects were considered: A) environmental impacts arising from the conventional management of the biowastes; B) impact sav- ings of substituted services due to the fact that these biowastes are resources of interest for other activities. Therefore, it seems consis- tent that, for the biogas system expansion, substituting conventional management by the anaerobic digestion involves environmental savings from budget A, but the environmental attributions for bud- get B.

Emissions belonging to actions included in budget A include CH4 and N20 discharges caused by temporary storage of pig/cow manure in an uncovered tank before field application as fertilizer and also by transport of all biowaste materials used by conventional means. To determine these issues the following assumptions were made:

CH4 and N20 emissions caused by stored pig/cow manure were cal- culated according to the report produced by the Joint Research Cen- ter (Giuntoli et al., 2014). This report describes the assumptions made by the JRC when compiling default and typical GHG emissions for different solid and gaseous bioenergy pathways. The calculations made in this report are in accordance with the methodology set in COM (2010) (Commission, 2010a) and SWD (2014) 259 (Commission, 2014) in order to help economic operators to declare GHG emission savings associated with the production of electricity, heating and cooling from solid and gaseous biomass

sources. COM (2010) states sustainability criteria for solid and gas- eous bioenergy pathways which are similar to those reported in the Renewable Energy Directive (Commission, 2009a) and the Fuel Quality Directive (Commission, 2009b) for calculating the green- house impact of biofuels, bioliquids and their fossil fuels comparators.

The JRC report (Giuntoli et al., 2014) states that anaerobic digestion of manure should be considered as an improved agricultural manage- ment practice and that emissions derived from its storage must be con- sidered as a credit to the biogas pathway. This approach is in accordance with the system expansion proposed in this investigation by the authors.

Based on IPCC Guidelines, the ratio between methane emissions caused by the storage of manure and the storage of digestate is given by the reduction of volatile solids during digestion. This implies that, with the specific conditions assumed in this study (VS reduction = 43%), the credits methane emissions from the stored manure would be equal to 1/0.57 = 1.75 times the emissions from digestate storage. Considering that methane emission from the digestate are equal to 10.0% of the meth- ane produced at the biogas plant, the overall credits would be equal to 17.5% of the methane produced, i.e. 3.5 g CH4/MJ biogas.

Concerning N20 emissions, it has been considered that the propor- tion of ammoniacal nitrogen in the digestate is supposed to increase and that the total N is decreased due to losses in the digester, it is as- sumed that the net emissions from manure and digestate are equal and thus the credit would simply balance out the N20 emissions assigned to digestate storage. Thus, N20 avoided emission applied in this study was equal to 0.066 gN2O/MJ biogas.

Considering that methane emission from the digestate are equal to 10.0% of the meth- ane produced at the biogas plant, the overall credits would be equal to 17.5% of the methane produced, i.e. 3.5 g CH4/MJ biogas.

Concerning N20 emissions, it has been considered that the propor- tion of ammoniacal nitrogen in the digestate is supposed to increase and that the total N is decreased due to losses in the digester, it is as- sumed that the net emissions from manure and digestate are equal and thus the credit would simply balance out the N20 emissions assigned to digestate storage. Thus, N20 avoided emission applied in this study was equal to 0.066 gN2O/MJ biogas.
20: <header>2.2.1. Waste transport </header>

As explained above, the biogas plant accepts different types of bio- logical residues. Burden allocation for the biogas substrates was made according to the Polluter Pays Principle which assumes no environmental burdens for waste materials.

Table 2 shows the biowaste inputs and the assumptions employed to simulate the environmental burdens associated with transporting the different wastes to the biogas plant. Activity data was calculated taking into consideration the loading capacity of the trucks (payload) and also the utilization ratios (workload), which were determined considering the transportation capacity of the cargos and the bulk density of the wastes. For the sewage sludge and the agri-food fractions, an empty re- turn of the trucks was assumed in the analyses. Tractors employed to transport the pig/cow manure were assumed to return with digestate. This activity data was applied to life cycle inventories from the European Life Cycle Database (ELCD 3.1), which include impacts attrib- utable to fuel production and combustion. Emissions due to tyre abra- sion, vehicle fabrication and maintenance, and construction of road
Table 2
Waste inputs and digestate outputs per functional unit of the biogas plant including type, origin and transportation requirements.

Feedstock|||Transport|Transport|Transport|Transport Origin||kg per 1 kWh|Vehicle|Round trip (km)|Load in departure (t)|Load in returnb (t) Pig manure|Livestock husbandry|3.25|Tractor|40|6|6 Cow manure||3.25|Tractor|40|6|6 Sewage sludge|Municipal waste water and from slaughterhouse|3.60|Truck, 22 t|60|14|0 Agri-food wastes|Fruit and vegetables, slaughterhouse and expired food|2.90|Truck, 40 t|120|20|0
a Weighted average from different origins. b Digestate output for farmers.

infrastructures were also considered (Spielmann et al., 2007). Transport distances for each biogas substrate were calculated considering the lo- cation of the biowaste provider and also the mass supplied.
2.2.2. Operation and maintenance

This phase covers all the activities related to the operation and main- tenance (O&M) of the biogas plant including biowaste reception and biogas production. Table 3 describes in quantitative terms the main in- puts/outputs resulting from this life cycle phase, referenced to a func- tional unit (1 kWh). Activity data associated with the consumption of power, water and lubricants, as well as the generation of digestate and hazardous and no hazardous wastes, were quantified using a top/ down approach. This involved analyzing the internal accounting system of the biogas plant. Material and energy flows were divided by the aver- age biogas production of the plant in order to determine activity data per functional unit of product.

Power consumed onsite the biogas plant was assumed to be ob- tained from the grid. The electricity mix for purchased power was that of Spain in 2014 (REE, 2014). CH4 and N20 emissions from stored digestate in open tanks have been calculated considering the guidelines produced by the JRC (Giuntoli et al., 2014). Emission of unburnt meth- ane in biogas fueled engines, known as methane slip, is included in the analysis assuming the conventional values reported by the JRC (Giuntoli et al., 2014).

Impacts associated with land occupation of the biogas plant (0.8 have been shared by all the biogas produced throughout its lifetime. Emission factors for water, electricity and lubricant consumed in the biogas plant were obtained from Ecoinvent v3.1 database.
2.2.3. The biogas plant

This life cycle phase includes the raw materials and activities associ- ated with the construction and decommissioning of the biogas plant, its components and also the end of life (EoL) of all the materials employed in these two stages. Table 4 provides the inventory data associated with Construction and Dismantling (C&D) activities and Table 5 describes the inventories regarding the Equipment and Machinery (E&M) employed in the biogas plant. The environmental burdens calculated for each of the elements included in these two sections have been shared by all the biogas generated during the lifetime of the plant. Life cycle invento- ries for construction activities, materials and equipment have been ob- tained from Ecoinvent v3.1 datasets.

In particular, inventory data for the manufacturing of the cogenera- tion unit has been adapted from Ecoinvent v3.1 datasets specific for this type of equipment. Emissions for the manufacturing of items such as stirrers or pumps have been inventoried using a generic dataset for "heavy industrial machinery" using mass as reference. The number of units considered for each of the plant components has been calculated taking into account its life expectancy and that of the plant (20 years).
2.2.4. Avoided impacts

As stated above, in the absence of the biogas plant, the biowaste ma- terials would have followed conventional waste management routes leading to different functions and environmental burdens. A system ex- pansion approach was applied to include in the analysis the additional functions of the biogas system. Thus, all impacts attributable to biowastes transport, operation and maintenance, and construction and dismantling of the biogas plant have been allocated to the electricity

generated. Environmental burdens associated with the products ob- tained from the conventional management of these residues were cal- culated separately and subtracted from those determined for the generation of biogas power.

In order to quantify the environmental burdens associated with the conventional management routes of the biowastes, two impor- tant aspects were considered: A) environmental impacts arising from the conventional management of the biowastes; B) impact sav- ings of substituted services due to the fact that these biowastes are resources of interest for other activities. Therefore, it seems consis- tent that, for the biogas system expansion, substituting conventional management by the anaerobic digestion involves environmental savings from budget A, but the environmental attributions for bud- get B.

Emissions belonging to actions included in budget A include CH4 and N20 discharges caused by temporary storage of pig/cow manure in an uncovered tank before field application as fertilizer and also by transport of all biowaste materials used by conventional means. To determine these issues the following assumptions were made:

CH4 and N20 emissions caused by stored pig/cow manure were cal- culated according to the report produced by the Joint Research Cen- ter (Giuntoli et al., 2014). This report describes the assumptions made by the JRC when compiling default and typical GHG emissions for different solid and gaseous bioenergy pathways. The calculations made in this report are in accordance with the methodology set in COM (2010) (Commission, 2010a) and SWD (2014) 259 (Commission, 2014) in order to help economic operators to declare GHG emission savings associated with the production of electricity, heating and cooling from solid and gaseous biomass

sources. COM (2010) states sustainability criteria for solid and gas- eous bioenergy pathways which are similar to those reported in the Renewable Energy Directive (Commission, 2009a) and the Fuel Quality Directive (Commission, 2009b) for calculating the green- house impact of biofuels, bioliquids and their fossil fuels comparators.

The JRC report (Giuntoli et al., 2014) states that anaerobic digestion of manure should be considered as an improved agricultural manage- ment practice and that emissions derived from its storage must be con- sidered as a credit to the biogas pathway. This approach is in accordance with the system expansion proposed in this investigation by the authors.

Based on IPCC Guidelines, the ratio between methane emissions caused by the storage of manure and the storage of digestate is given by the reduction of volatile solids during digestion. This implies that, with the specific conditions assumed in this study (VS reduction = 43%), the credits methane emissions from the stored manure would be equal to 1/0.57 = 1.75 times the emissions from digestate storage. Considering that methane emission from the digestate are equal to 10.0% of the meth- ane produced at the biogas plant, the overall credits would be equal to 17.5% of the methane produced, i.e. 3.5 g CH4/MJ biogas.

Concerning N20 emissions, it has been considered that the propor- tion of ammoniacal nitrogen in the digestate is supposed to increase and that the total N is decreased due to losses in the digester, it is as- sumed that the net emissions from manure and digestate are equal and thus the credit would simply balance out the N20 emissions assigned to digestate storage. Thus, N20 avoided emission applied in this study was equal to 0.066 gN2O/MJ biogas.

COM (2010) states sustainability criteria for solid and gas- eous bioenergy pathways which are similar to those reported in the Renewable Energy Directive (Commission, 2009a) and the Fuel Quality Directive (Commission, 2009b) for calculating the green- house impact of biofuels, bioliquids and their fossil fuels comparators.

The JRC report (Giuntoli et al., 2014) states that anaerobic digestion of manure should be considered as an improved agricultural manage- ment practice and that emissions derived from its storage must be con- sidered as a credit to the biogas pathway. This approach is in accordance with the system expansion proposed in this investigation by the authors.

Based on IPCC Guidelines, the ratio between methane emissions caused by the storage of manure and the storage of digestate is given by the reduction of volatile solids during digestion. This implies that, with the specific conditions assumed in this study (VS reduction = 43%), the credits methane emissions from the stored manure would be equal to 1/0.57 = 1.75 times the emissions from digestate storage. Considering that methane emission from the digestate are equal to 10.0% of the meth- ane produced at the biogas plant, the overall credits would be equal to 17.5% of the methane produced, i.e. 3.5 g CH4/MJ biogas.

Concerning N20 emissions, it has been considered that the propor- tion of ammoniacal nitrogen in the digestate is supposed to increase and that the total N is decreased due to losses in the digester, it is as- sumed that the net emissions from manure and digestate are equal and thus the credit would simply balance out the N20 emissions assigned to digestate storage. Thus, N20 avoided emission applied in this study was equal to 0.066 gN2O/MJ biogas.
23: <header>2.2.1. Waste transport </header>

As explained above, the biogas plant accepts different types of bio- logical residues. Burden allocation for the biogas substrates was made according to the Polluter Pays Principle which assumes no environmental burdens for waste materials.

Table 2 shows the biowaste inputs and the assumptions employed to simulate the environmental burdens associated with transporting the different wastes to the biogas plant. Activity data was calculated taking into consideration the loading capacity of the trucks (payload) and also the utilization ratios (workload), which were determined considering the transportation capacity of the cargos and the bulk density of the wastes. For the sewage sludge and the agri-food fractions, an empty re- turn of the trucks was assumed in the analyses. Tractors employed to transport the pig/cow manure were assumed to return with digestate. This activity data was applied to life cycle inventories from the European Life Cycle Database (ELCD 3.1), which include impacts attrib- utable to fuel production and combustion. Emissions due to tyre abra- sion, vehicle fabrication and maintenance, and construction of road
Table 2
Waste inputs and digestate outputs per functional unit of the biogas plant including type, origin and transportation requirements.

Feedstock|||Transport|Transport|Transport|Transport Origin||kg per 1 kWh|Vehicle|Round trip (km)|Load in departure (t)|Load in returnb (t) Pig manure|Livestock husbandry|3.25|Tractor|40|6|6 Cow manure||3.25|Tractor|40|6|6 Sewage sludge|Municipal waste water and from slaughterhouse|3.60|Truck, 22 t|60|14|0 Agri-food wastes|Fruit and vegetables, slaughterhouse and expired food|2.90|Truck, 40 t|120|20|0
a Weighted average from different origins. b Digestate output for farmers.

infrastructures were also considered (Spielmann et al., 2007). Transport distances for each biogas substrate were calculated considering the lo- cation of the biowaste provider and also the mass supplied.
2.2.2. Operation and maintenance

This phase covers all the activities related to the operation and main- tenance (O&M) of the biogas plant including biowaste reception and biogas production. Table 3 describes in quantitative terms the main in- puts/outputs resulting from this life cycle phase, referenced to a func- tional unit (1 kWh). Activity data associated with the consumption of power, water and lubricants, as well as the generation of digestate and hazardous and no hazardous wastes, were quantified using a top/ down approach. This involved analyzing the internal accounting system of the biogas plant. Material and energy flows were divided by the aver- age biogas production of the plant in order to determine activity data per functional unit of product.

Power consumed onsite the biogas plant was assumed to be ob- tained from the grid. The electricity mix for purchased power was that of Spain in 2014 (REE, 2014). CH4 and N20 emissions from stored digestate in open tanks have been calculated considering the guidelines produced by the JRC (Giuntoli et al., 2014). Emission of unburnt meth- ane in biogas fueled engines, known as methane slip, is included in the analysis assuming the conventional values reported by the JRC (Giuntoli et al., 2014).

Impacts associated with land occupation of the biogas plant (0.8 have been shared by all the biogas produced throughout its lifetime. Emission factors for water, electricity and lubricant consumed in the biogas plant were obtained from Ecoinvent v3.1 database.
2.2.3. The biogas plant

This life cycle phase includes the raw materials and activities associ- ated with the construction and decommissioning of the biogas plant, its components and also the end of life (EoL) of all the materials employed in these two stages. Table 4 provides the inventory data associated with Construction and Dismantling (C&D) activities and Table 5 describes the inventories regarding the Equipment and Machinery (E&M) employed in the biogas plant. The environmental burdens calculated for each of the elements included in these two sections have been shared by all the biogas generated during the lifetime of the plant. Life cycle invento- ries for construction activities, materials and equipment have been ob- tained from Ecoinvent v3.1 datasets.

In particular, inventory data for the manufacturing of the cogenera- tion unit has been adapted from Ecoinvent v3.1 datasets specific for this type of equipment. Emissions for the manufacturing of items such as stirrers or pumps have been inventoried using a generic dataset for "heavy industrial machinery" using mass as reference. The number of units considered for each of the plant components has been calculated taking into account its life expectancy and that of the plant (20 years).
2.2.4. Avoided impacts

As stated above, in the absence of the biogas plant, the biowaste ma- terials would have followed conventional waste management routes leading to different functions and environmental burdens. A system ex- pansion approach was applied to include in the analysis the additional functions of the biogas system. Thus, all impacts attributable to biowastes transport, operation and maintenance, and construction and dismantling of the biogas plant have been allocated to the electricity

generated. Environmental burdens associated with the products ob- tained from the conventional management of these residues were cal- culated separately and subtracted from those determined for the generation of biogas power.

In order to quantify the environmental burdens associated with the conventional management routes of the biowastes, two impor- tant aspects were considered: A) environmental impacts arising from the conventional management of the biowastes; B) impact sav- ings of substituted services due to the fact that these biowastes are resources of interest for other activities. Therefore, it seems consis- tent that, for the biogas system expansion, substituting conventional management by the anaerobic digestion involves environmental savings from budget A, but the environmental attributions for bud- get B.

Emissions belonging to actions included in budget A include CH4 and N20 discharges caused by temporary storage of pig/cow manure in an uncovered tank before field application as fertilizer and also by transport of all biowaste materials used by conventional means. To determine these issues the following assumptions were made:

CH4 and N20 emissions caused by stored pig/cow manure were cal- culated according to the report produced by the Joint Research Cen- ter (Giuntoli et al., 2014). This report describes the assumptions made by the JRC when compiling default and typical GHG emissions for different solid and gaseous bioenergy pathways. The calculations made in this report are in accordance with the methodology set in COM (2010) (Commission, 2010a) and SWD (2014) 259 (Commission, 2014) in order to help economic operators to declare GHG emission savings associated with the production of electricity, heating and cooling from solid and gaseous biomass

sources. COM (2010) states sustainability criteria for solid and gas- eous bioenergy pathways which are similar to those reported in the Renewable Energy Directive (Commission, 2009a) and the Fuel Quality Directive (Commission, 2009b) for calculating the green- house impact of biofuels, bioliquids and their fossil fuels comparators.

The JRC report (Giuntoli et al., 2014) states that anaerobic digestion of manure should be considered as an improved agricultural manage- ment practice and that emissions derived from its storage must be con- sidered as a credit to the biogas pathway. This approach is in accordance with the system expansion proposed in this investigation by the authors.

Based on IPCC Guidelines, the ratio between methane emissions caused by the storage of manure and the storage of digestate is given by the reduction of volatile solids during digestion. This implies that, with the specific conditions assumed in this study (VS reduction = 43%), the credits methane emissions from the stored manure would be equal to 1/0.57 = 1.75 times the emissions from digestate storage. Considering that methane emission from the digestate are equal to 10.0% of the meth- ane produced at the biogas plant, the overall credits would be equal to 17.5% of the methane produced, i.e. 3.5 g CH4/MJ biogas.

Concerning N20 emissions, it has been considered that the propor- tion of ammoniacal nitrogen in the digestate is supposed to increase and that the total N is decreased due to losses in the digester, it is as- sumed that the net emissions from manure and digestate are equal and thus the credit would simply balance out the N20 emissions assigned to digestate storage. Thus, N20 avoided emission applied in this study was equal to 0.066 gN2O/MJ biogas.
26: <header>2.2.1. Waste transport </header>

As explained above, the biogas plant accepts different types of bio- logical residues. Burden allocation for the biogas substrates was made according to the Polluter Pays Principle which assumes no environmental burdens for waste materials.

Table 2 shows the biowaste inputs and the assumptions employed to simulate the environmental burdens associated with transporting the different wastes to the biogas plant. Activity data was calculated taking into consideration the loading capacity of the trucks (payload) and also the utilization ratios (workload), which were determined considering the transportation capacity of the cargos and the bulk density of the wastes. For the sewage sludge and the agri-food fractions, an empty re- turn of the trucks was assumed in the analyses. Tractors employed to transport the pig/cow manure were assumed to return with digestate. This activity data was applied to life cycle inventories from the European Life Cycle Database (ELCD 3.1), which include impacts attrib- utable to fuel production and combustion. Emissions due to tyre abra- sion, vehicle fabrication and maintenance, and construction of road

infrastructures were also considered (Spielmann et al., 2007). Transport distances for each biogas substrate were calculated considering the lo- cation of the biowaste provider and also the mass supplied.
2.2.2. Operation and maintenance

This phase covers all the activities related to the operation and main- tenance (O&M) of the biogas plant including biowaste reception and biogas production. Table 3 describes in quantitative terms the main in- puts/outputs resulting from this life cycle phase, referenced to a func- tional unit (1 kWh). Activity data associated with the consumption of power, water and lubricants, as well as the generation of digestate and hazardous and no hazardous wastes, were quantified using a top/ down approach. This involved analyzing the internal accounting system of the biogas plant. Material and energy flows were divided by the aver- age biogas production of the plant in order to determine activity data per functional unit of product.

Power consumed onsite the biogas plant was assumed to be ob- tained from the grid. The electricity mix for purchased power was that of Spain in 2014 (REE, 2014). CH4 and N20 emissions from stored digestate in open tanks have been calculated considering the guidelines produced by the JRC (Giuntoli et al., 2014). Emission of unburnt meth- ane in biogas fueled engines, known as methane slip, is included in the analysis assuming the conventional values reported by the JRC (Giuntoli et al., 2014).

Impacts associated with land occupation of the biogas plant (0.8 have been shared by all the biogas produced throughout its lifetime. Emission factors for water, electricity and lubricant consumed in the biogas plant were obtained from Ecoinvent v3.1 database.
2.2.3. The biogas plant

This life cycle phase includes the raw materials and activities associ- ated with the construction and decommissioning of the biogas plant, its components and also the end of life (EoL) of all the materials employed in these two stages. Table 4 provides the inventory data associated with Construction and Dismantling (C&D) activities and Table 5 describes the inventories regarding the Equipment and Machinery (E&M) employed in the biogas plant. The environmental burdens calculated for each of the elements included in these two sections have been shared by all the biogas generated during the lifetime of the plant. Life cycle invento- ries for construction activities, materials and equipment have been ob- tained from Ecoinvent v3.1 datasets.

In particular, inventory data for the manufacturing of the cogenera- tion unit has been adapted from Ecoinvent v3.1 datasets specific for this type of equipment. Emissions for the manufacturing of items such as stirrers or pumps have been inventoried using a generic dataset for "heavy industrial machinery" using mass as reference. The number of units considered for each of the plant components has been calculated taking into account its life expectancy and that of the plant (20 years).
2.2.4. Avoided impacts

As stated above, in the absence of the biogas plant, the biowaste ma- terials would have followed conventional waste management routes leading to different functions and environmental burdens. A system ex- pansion approach was applied to include in the analysis the additional functions of the biogas system. Thus, all impacts attributable to biowastes transport, operation and maintenance, and construction and dismantling of the biogas plant have been allocated to the electricity

Transport to EoL in conventional management of biowastes (used as fertilizer and as animal feed) has been included, assuming an aver- age distance of 25 km.

To determine emissions associated with budget B, it was taken into account that the conventional treatment of biowastes under analysis can substitute the services of: i) fertilization in the case of pig/cow ma- nure and sewage sludge, and ii) animal feed in the case of agri-food waste (both shown in Fig. 1). Field fertilization with sewage sludge and pig/cow manure can be excluded of system expansion because the digestate from these biowastes fractions would be also applied with the same purpose in the biogas pathway. The same fertilization ca- pacity may be assumed for both the digested and the non digested biowastes since nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium contents are not affected by the anaerobic digestion. Finally, the service given by agri- food waste fraction as animal feed has been also excluded of the system expansion due to the difficulties and uncertainties associated with de- fining a functional unit based on nutritional value and also the lack of in- formation about the precise composition of this biowaste fraction. Therefore, only emissions from the budget A (avoided impacts) have been deduced and included in the system expansion of the biogas pathway.

Transport to EoL in conventional management of biowastes (used as fertilizer and as animal feed) has been included, assuming an aver- age distance of 25 km.

To determine emissions associated with budget B, it was taken into account that the conventional treatment of biowastes under analysis can substitute the services of: i) fertilization in the case of pig/cow ma- nure and sewage sludge, and ii) animal feed in the case of agri-food waste (both shown in Fig. 1). Field fertilization with sewage sludge and pig/cow manure can be excluded of system expansion because the digestate from these biowastes fractions would be also applied with the same purpose in the biogas pathway. The same fertilization ca- pacity may be assumed for both the digested and the non digested biowastes since nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium contents are not affected by the anaerobic digestion. Finally, the service given by agri- food waste fraction as animal feed has been also excluded of the system expansion due to the difficulties and uncertainties associated with de- fining a functional unit based on nutritional value and also the lack of in- formation about the precise composition of this biowaste fraction. Therefore, only emissions from the budget A (avoided impacts) have been deduced and included in the system expansion of the biogas pathway.

To determine emissions associated with budget B, it was taken into account that the conventional treatment of biowastes under analysis can substitute the services of: i) fertilization in the case of pig/cow ma- nure and sewage sludge, and ii) animal feed in the case of agri-food waste (both shown in Fig. 1). Field fertilization with sewage sludge and pig/cow manure can be excluded of system expansion because the digestate from these biowastes fractions would be also applied with the same purpose in the biogas pathway. The same fertilization ca- pacity may be assumed for both the digested and the non digested biowastes since nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium contents are not affected by the anaerobic digestion. Finally, the service given by agri- food waste fraction as animal feed has been also excluded of the system expansion due to the difficulties and uncertainties associated with de- fining a functional unit based on nutritional value and also the lack of in- formation about the precise composition of this biowaste fraction. Therefore, only emissions from the budget A (avoided impacts) have been deduced and included in the system expansion of the biogas pathway.
32: The digestate generated by the biogas plant is returned to the envi- ronment in the form of fertilizer, which is offered to local farmers free of charge. Despite not having an economic value, the use of digestate as fertilizer may avoid the consumption of commercial fertilizers. Hence, this could be considered as an additional function of the biogas system (production of fertilizers), requiring the allocation of some of the envi- ronmental burden of the biogas system. As discussed above, allocation should be avoided as far as possible with a system expansion, deducing from the biogas system the environmental burdens associated with the surrogate fertilizers. As stated in Section 2.2.4, sewage sludge and pig/ cow manure would also be used as fertilizer in the conventional man- agement of these biowastes. Therefore, the amount of surrogate fertil- izers produced by the biogas system should correspond only to the digestate fraction originated from agri-food wastes. Thus, the environ- mental profile of the biogas system in the base case scenario was com- pared with an alternative scenario where the digestate produced by the agri-food biowaste fraction is used as a surrogate fertilizer with the same amounts of nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium. In this case, aspects such as the actual amount of nitrogen available in the soil from the digestate or its mineralization to produce compounds as- similable by crops have not been taken into account. The amount of digestate produced by the agri-food waste fraction has been calculated considering its mass contribution to the total substrate inputs and the digestate outputs generated by the plant.
33: The LCA was performed according to ISO 14040 and ISO 14044, and using the ILCD methodology published by the Joint Research Centre of the European Commission (EC-JRC) (Commission, 2010b) in the follow- ing categories: climate change, ozone depletion, photochemical ozone formation, particulate matter, ionizing radiation, acidification, eutrophi- cation, human toxicity, ecotoxicity, land use and resource depletion (water, mineral, fossil and renewable). Only midpoint indicators have been calculated in this work due to the questions posed by the scientific community regarding robustness of endpoint indicators (Commission, 2010b; Sala et al., 2012). Normalisation includes the emissions and the resource extracted occurring within the EU-27 boundaries (domestic inventory) for the year 2010 (Benini et al., 2014). Cumulative Energy Demand (CED), which describes the primary energy consumed throughout the life cycle of the system, was calculated using CED
v1.08 (Hischier and Weidema, 2010). SimaPro v8.1 software was used to build the models and calculations.
34: <header>2.4. Life cycle inventory of the economic analysis </header>

The economic analysis was performed considering the producer per- spective. Under this assumption, the producer bears the costs associated with the construction, operation and dismantling of the biogas plant. All the costs and revenues considered in this analysis were provided by the producer. The economic inventory was organized considering the same four life cycle phases defined in the environmental LCA as follows: a) waste transport, b) operation & maintenance, c) biogas plant, and d) avoided impacts.

The waste transport phase is included in order to maintain coher- ence with the LCA. However, it does not involve any expenses for the producer, since it is a free service offered by the waste producers. The biogas plant phase includes construction of the biogas plant (taking place during the first year of the project) and dismantling (taking place in the last year of the project). The operation & maintenance phase takes place after the construction of the biogas plant and lasts for 20 years. It comprises costs attributable to personnel, maintenance activities (repairing, testing, cleaning...) water purchase and electricity consumption. The avoided impacts phase does not include any costs borne by the producer. However, the biogas plant benefits from public subsidies from the CLIMA Program (MAPAMA, 2017) due to carbon emissions savings attributable to the operation of the biogas plant (as compared with conventional waste management practices for the biowastes). The amount of this public subsidy is proportional to the avoided emissions and it is calculated on an annual basis, applicable to the first four years of operation of the biogas plant. The revenues from electricity sale are calculated considering an average price of 5.03 C €/kWh, according to the average annual price for 2015 published by OMIE (operator of the Iberian electricity market). Considered costs in this analysis are represented in €2015 and summarized in Table 6.

This economic analysis applies a "producer perspective" where all the costs and revenues are attributable to elec- tricity generation, without considering actual or potential uses of the waste substrates.

Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR) were also used as indicators of the economic viability of the biogas project (con- sidering both waste management and electricity). The discount rate adopted for all internal cash flows is 7.398%. This value is the same as the "reasonable return on investment" for renewable energy projects susceptible for public support as adopted by the Spanish Ministry of Industry and Energy (BOE, 2014). This discount rate takes into account both opportunity costs and risk premium. IRR was calcu- lated as the discount rate at which NPV equals zero. Monetary values were actualized using Eq. (1):

39: 3.1. Environmental analysis of biogas production: base case VS alternative scenario

Table 8 shows the impacts associated with the generation of a func- tional unit of the biogas system (1 kWh of electricity). The results have been obtained for the scenarios referred to as base case and alternative scenario. Twelve categories have been included in these analyses, which represent the most relevant in terms of environmental significance. The results show a negative impact value of -73.9 CO2 eq/kWh for the cli- mate change category in the base case scenario. This means that avoided CO2 and N20 emissions associated with the storage of pig/cow manure (conventional management) more than offset GHG emissions of the biogas system. The Net Energy Ratio (NER) of the biogas system, i.e., the fraction between the net energy generated (functional unit, 1 kWh) and consumed by the biogas system (CED), is 1.081.
As shown in Table 8, the use of the digestate generated by the agri- food waste as a fertilizer represents a significant improvement in all

the environmental categories of the biogas system. Thus, the negative value observed on the climate change category increases even further to 174.3 g CO2 eq/kWh due to emissions savings associated with the use of the digestate as a surrogate fertilizer. Taking the base case sce- nario as a reference, reduced environmental burdens in other categories are as follows: ozone depletion (32%), human toxicity (14%), particulate matter (49%), ionizing radiation (16%), photochemical ozone formation (27%), acidification (38%), freshwater eutrophication (24%), freshwater ecotoxicity (24%), land use (60%), water resource depletion (25%) and CED (30%). The NER calculated for the alternative scenario is signifi- cantly higher at 1.545.

Fig. 2 shows the contribution of each one of the four life cycle phases considered in this analysis (waste transport, operation & maintenance, biogas plant and avoided impacts) to the environmental impacts se- lected for base case scenario. The graph shows that the most impacting phase in all impacts categories is operation & maintenance, except for water resource depletion (whose main contribution is attributable to the waste transport phase) and climate change (due to avoided im- pacts). The second most important contribution relates to the waste transport phase on the impacts categories of CED (23.8%), land use (21.7%), acidification (14.2%), particulate matter (22.6%) and ozone de- pletion (11.5%). The life cycle phase identified as biogas plant has a lower significance in the impacts categories of freshwater ecotoxicity (15.6%), freshwater eutrophication (7.5%), ionizing radiation (6.2%) and human toxicity (14.3%). The avoided impacts phase is the most rel- evant not only in the climate change category (52.5%) but also in water depletion (33.3%) and photochemical ozone formation (23.9%).

Being operation & maintenance (O&M) the life cycle phase contrib- uting the most to the environmental performance of the biogas system, a detailed analysis of this phase has been carried out. Fig. 3 shows the contribution of different emission sources to the O&M phase of the base case scenario. The results show that impact on the climate change category is mainly due to digestate storage (51.1%) and methane slip (20.3%). Purchased electricity, biogas combustion and internal transport contribute with 10.5%, 9.3% and 7.6% respectively. For CED, the main contributions are the energy consumed for the production of purchased electricity (62.2%) and for internal transport (31.3%). The impact cate- gories of water resource depletion and land use are affected mainly by direct water consumption and occupied surface area of biogas plant (87% and 43% respectively). For the rest of impact categories, the most important sources of environmental burdens are related to biogas com- bustion, purchased electricity and internal transport.

Biogas combustion generates 79.2% of the impact associated with the freshwater eutrophication category, 78.1% of freshwater ecotoxicity, 50.4% of acidification, 33.7% for particulate matter, 33.1% of photochem- ical ozone formation, 31.0% of human toxicity, 11.0% for ozone depletion and 9.3% for climate change.
Purchased electricity is the main contributor to emissions in the ion- izing radiation category within the operation & maintenance phase
Fig. 2. Contribution of different life cycle phases to the characterized impacts of the biogas plant (base case scenario).
(88.7%) and has important weight on ozone depletion (49.7%), acidifica- tion (24.8%), particulate matter (23.9%) and photochemical ozone for- mation (19.2%).

Internal transport has a significant contribution to the operation & maintenance phase in the cases of human toxicity (63.9%), particulate matter (39.2%), photochemical ozone formation (35.2%), ozone deple- tion (28.6%) and acidification (22.8%). For freshwater ecotoxicity, fresh- water eutrophication and ionizing radiation the contribution of purchased electricity and internal transport is lower than 14%.

Fig. 4 illustrates the normalized and weighted impact values for the two scenarios considered in this investigation and also the contribution of each of the impact categories considered. The impact category most significantly affected in normalized terms in both scenarios is, by far, human toxicity. The second most affected category is freshwater ecotoxicity followed by freshwater eutrophication. Acidification, fresh- water eutrophication, photochemical ozone formation, particulate mat- ter and ionizing radiation are comparatively less significant in all scenarios. Finally, normalized impacts in the climate change, ozone
depletion, water resource depletion and land use categories are practi- cally insignificant in both scenarios.

The normalized results in Fig. 4 illustrate that local impact categories are the most significantly affected by the biogas system (primarily human toxicity, fresh water ecotoxicity, particulate matter). In contrast, global impact categories (such as climate change and ozone depletion) are less severely affected. It is noteworthy that normalized results are highly dependent on the methodology employed (Heijungs et al., 2007), ILCD in this case, and may condition the environmental profile of the system with respect to the characterized results.

Acidification, fresh- water eutrophication, photochemical ozone formation, particulate mat- ter and ionizing radiation are comparatively less significant in all scenarios. Finally, normalized impacts in the climate change, ozone
depletion, water resource depletion and land use categories are practi- cally insignificant in both scenarios.

The normalized results in Fig. 4 illustrate that local impact categories are the most significantly affected by the biogas system (primarily human toxicity, fresh water ecotoxicity, particulate matter). In contrast, global impact categories (such as climate change and ozone depletion) are less severely affected. It is noteworthy that normalized results are highly dependent on the methodology employed (Heijungs et al., 2007), ILCD in this case, and may condition the environmental profile of the system with respect to the characterized results.

Internal cost sub-categories (in €)|Description Personnel costs|Blue-collar worker costs during operation of the power plant. Equipment and Machinery costs|Purchase of plant components including biogas reactors, pre-treatment installations, cogeneration system, biogas upgrading, electricity installations, valves, piping and pumps. Construction costs|Costs associated with construction and dismantling of the biogas plant. and machinery.
