the environmental categories of the biogas system. Thus, the negative value observed on the climate change category increases even further to 174.3 g CO2 eq/kWh due to emissions savings associated with the use of the digestate as a surrogate fertilizer. Taking the base case sce- nario as a reference, reduced environmental burdens in other categories are as follows: ozone depletion (32%), human toxicity (14%), particulate matter (49%), ionizing radiation (16%), photochemical ozone formation (27%), acidification (38%), freshwater eutrophication (24%), freshwater ecotoxicity (24%), land use (60%), water resource depletion (25%) and CED (30%). The NER calculated for the alternative scenario is signifi- cantly higher at 1.545.

Fig. 2 shows the contribution of each one of the four life cycle phases considered in this analysis (waste transport, operation & maintenance, biogas plant and avoided impacts) to the environmental impacts se- lected for base case scenario. The graph shows that the most impacting phase in all impacts categories is operation & maintenance, except for water resource depletion (whose main contribution is attributable to the waste transport phase) and climate change (due to avoided im- pacts). The second most important contribution relates to the waste transport phase on the impacts categories of CED (23.8%), land use (21.7%), acidification (14.2%), particulate matter (22.6%) and ozone de- pletion (11.5%). The life cycle phase identified as biogas plant has a lower significance in the impacts categories of freshwater ecotoxicity (15.6%), freshwater eutrophication (7.5%), ionizing radiation (6.2%) and human toxicity (14.3%). The avoided impacts phase is the most rel- evant not only in the climate change category (52.5%) but also in water depletion (33.3%) and photochemical ozone formation (23.9%).

Being operation & maintenance (O&M) the life cycle phase contrib- uting the most to the environmental performance of the biogas system, a detailed analysis of this phase has been carried out. Fig. 3 shows the contribution of different emission sources to the O&M phase of the base case scenario. The results show that impact on the climate change category is mainly due to digestate storage (51.1%) and methane slip (20.3%). Purchased electricity, biogas combustion and internal transport contribute with 10.5%, 9.3% and 7.6% respectively. For CED, the main contributions are the energy consumed for the production of purchased electricity (62.2%) and for internal transport (31.3%). The impact cate- gories of water resource depletion and land use are affected mainly by direct water consumption and occupied surface area of biogas plant (87% and 43% respectively). For the rest of impact categories, the most important sources of environmental burdens are related to biogas com- bustion, purchased electricity and internal transport.

Biogas combustion generates 79.2% of the impact associated with the freshwater eutrophication category, 78.1% of freshwater ecotoxicity, 50.4% of acidification, 33.7% for particulate matter, 33.1% of photochem- ical ozone formation, 31.0% of human toxicity, 11.0% for ozone depletion and 9.3% for climate change.

(88.7%) and has important weight on ozone depletion (49.7%), acidifica- tion (24.8%), particulate matter (23.9%) and photochemical ozone for- mation (19.2%).

For freshwater ecotoxicity, fresh- water eutrophication and ionizing radiation the contribution of purchased electricity and internal transport is lower than 14%.

48: <header>3.2. Economic analysis of the biogas production </header>

Table 9 shows the internal costs per functional unit attributable to the biogas plant as determined by application of LCC methodology. To facilitate consistency in the comparative analysis of economic and envi- ronmental results, the life cycle phases considered in the LCC are the same as those considered in the LCA, although the waste transport
Fig. 3. Contribution of different emission sources to the Operation and Maintenance phase of the biogas plant (base case scenario).
Fig. 4. Aggregated normalized and weighted impacts on different environmental categories as determined for two scenarios under study.

phase have not been assigned any costs as this is a free service offered by the waste producers. The subsidies (revenues) received by the biogas plant from the CLIMA Programme have been assigned to the Avoided impact phase.

The results show that the total life cycle costs of the biogas system amounts to 17.6 c€/kWh, with the highest contribution corresponding to the O&M phase (10.3 c€/kWh, equivalent to 58.7% of the total) due primarily to personnel (3.95 c€/kWh) and also water and electricity ex- penses (1.72 c€/kWh) and maintenance (1.55 c€/kWh). The life cycle stage referred to as biogas plant contributes to 7.26 c€/kWh, which rep- resents 41.3% of all the life cycle costs of the system. The main processes in this phase are related to the purchase of Equipment and machinery (3.61 c€/kWh) followed by Construction (2.87 c€/kWh) and Land pur- chase costs (0.78 c€/kWh).

Total revenues amount to 22.5 c€/kWh (including corporate incom- ing tax), due to the gate fees charged for the management of the differ- ent organic wastes (80.7%) and electricity sales (17.4%). Subsidies from the CLIMA Programme only contribute to 2.08% of the life cycle revenues.

Fig. 5 shows the contribution of each life phase to the total costs of the biogas system in selected environmental categories. These profiles have been compared against the economic costs of the same biogas sys- tem, as determined using LCC. The environmental categories selected in this analysis were regarded as the most relevant in terms of environ- mental significance. Environmental savings resulting from avoiding conventional practices in the management of the biowastes due to the operation of the biogas plant are represented as negative values. These
Life cycle costs associated with the production of 1 kWh of electricity in the biogas plant (1.5% inflation).

Cost categories (c€/kWh)|Waste transport|O&M|Biogas plant|Avoided impacts|Total Personnel|0|3.95|0|0|3.95 Equipment and machinery|0|0|3.61|0|3.61 Construction|0|0|2.87|0|2.87 Land|0|0|0.78|0|0.78 Water and electricity|0|1.72|0|0|1.72 Maintenance|0|1.55|0|0|1.55 General costs and administration|0|1.29|0|0|1.29 Digestate transport|0|1.29|0|0|1.29 Insurance|0|0.52|0|0|0.52 Total costs (c€/kWh)|0|10.3|7.26|0|17.6 Revenues (c€/kWh)|0|22.5|0|0.48|23.0
have been compared with subsidies received by the biogas plant opera- tor from the public administration as compensation for externalities avoided by reducing greenhouse gas emissions.

As observed in the results, the environmental profile of the biogas system follows different outlines depending on the environmental cat- egory selected. Although the highest contribution lies in all impact cat- egories (except climate change) within the O&M phase, this may vary between 86.5% in the water eutrophication category and 52.0% in partic- ulate matter formation. Regarding climate change impacts, the environ- mental profile is dominated by the Avoided impacts phase due to impact savings resulting from the substitution of conventional biowaste management practices.

When comparing the economic and environmental profiles of the biogas system, some differences may be observed in the following as- pects. First, the life cycle phase referred to as Biogas plant has a signifi- cantly higher contribution in the economic analysis due to the high capital costs involved in the construction of the installation. Hence, it appears that the economic costs in this phase surpass the environmen- tal costs in relative terms. Consequently, the O&M has a proportionally lower economic significance while the contribution of the Avoided im- pacts phase is very limited, representing 2.7% of the total costs.

The waste transport phase does not have any repercussion in the life cycle costs. However, it represents between 4% and 24% of the total en- vironmental impacts in the selected categories. The biogas plant phase presents higher share of economic costs (28%) than environmental im- pacts (2-16%).

The NPV calculation for a discount rate of 7.398%, adopted by Spain's Ministry of Industry and Energy as a reasonable return on investment for renewable energy projects, amounts to 474,375 €, indicating that the bio- gas project, in the conditions described, is economically viable. The prof- itability of the project is primarily related to the income obtained from the gate fees charged for the management of the biowastes, which is three times higher than the income from electricity sale. As shown in Fig. 6, the NPV of the biogas plant is largely affected by the discount rate assumed in the calculation. The Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of the biogas project, which represents the discount rate at which the NPV of all cash flows equal to zero, is 10.2%, almost three points higher than the reasonable return of investment proposed by the Spain's Ministry.

54: The most impacting activities in the life cycle of the biogas system are primarily attributable to the operation & maintenance of the biogas
Fig. 5. Contribution of each life phase to the total life cycle costs of the biogas plant compared to the environmental contribution of selected impact categories.

plant. Excluding climate change and water resources depletion, the con- tribution of this phase to total impacts ranges between 49.4% for photo- chemical ozone formation and 86.5% for ionizing radiation and freshwater eutrophication. Among the emission sources belonging to operation & maintenance phase, the most impacting ones are biogas combustion, purchased electricity and internal transport. These sources have crucial effects on ozone depletion, human toxicity, particulate mat- ter, ionizing radiation, photochemical ozone formation, acidification, freshwater eutrophication, freshwater ecotoxicity and CED.

Avoided impacts from the substitution of conventional treatment by anaerobic digestion have an important influence on environmental bal- ance of biogas system. In the case of climate change, these avoided im- pacts offset the GHG emissions of rest of life cycle phases studied in this work.

Utilization of the digestate from agri-food waste fraction as a fertil- izer improves the environmental performance of the biogas system, pri- marily in the human toxicity and freshwater ecotoxicity, due primarily to savings in the emission of metals in the EoL stage.

LCC analysis revealed that the life cycle phase with highest share of economic costs is the O&M phase, followed by the biogas plant (con- struction and dismantling). The results suggest that the relative eco- nomic benefits associated with the GHG emissions avoidance are much lower than their environmental relative repercussion. The NPV results indicated that the project is economically viable, since the IRR obtained (10.2%) is higher than the considered reasonable return on in- vestment (7.398%). The profitability of the project is primarily related to
Fig. 6. NPV value for the biogas plant assuming different discount rates.

the income obtained from the gate fees charged for the management of the biowastes. The contribution of electricity sales to the economics of the biogas system is significantly lower while the public subsidies ob- tained from the CLIMA Program have a negligible repercussion.
55: The most impacting activities in the life cycle of the biogas system are primarily attributable to the operation & maintenance of the biogas

plant. Excluding climate change and water resources depletion, the con- tribution of this phase to total impacts ranges between 49.4% for photo- chemical ozone formation and 86.5% for ionizing radiation and freshwater eutrophication. Among the emission sources belonging to operation & maintenance phase, the most impacting ones are biogas combustion, purchased electricity and internal transport. These sources have crucial effects on ozone depletion, human toxicity, particulate mat- ter, ionizing radiation, photochemical ozone formation, acidification, freshwater eutrophication, freshwater ecotoxicity and CED.

Avoided impacts from the substitution of conventional treatment by anaerobic digestion have an important influence on environmental bal- ance of biogas system. In the case of climate change, these avoided im- pacts offset the GHG emissions of rest of life cycle phases studied in this work.

Utilization of the digestate from agri-food waste fraction as a fertil- izer improves the environmental performance of the biogas system, pri- marily in the human toxicity and freshwater ecotoxicity, due primarily to savings in the emission of metals in the EoL stage.

LCC analysis revealed that the life cycle phase with highest share of economic costs is the O&M phase, followed by the biogas plant (con- struction and dismantling). The results suggest that the relative eco- nomic benefits associated with the GHG emissions avoidance are much lower than their environmental relative repercussion. The NPV results indicated that the project is economically viable, since the IRR obtained (10.2%) is higher than the considered reasonable return on in- vestment (7.398%). The profitability of the project is primarily related to
Fig. 6. NPV value for the biogas plant assuming different discount rates.

the income obtained from the gate fees charged for the management of the biowastes. The contribution of electricity sales to the economics of the biogas system is significantly lower while the public subsidies ob- tained from the CLIMA Program have a negligible repercussion.
56: The most impacting activities in the life cycle of the biogas system are primarily attributable to the operation & maintenance of the biogas
Fig. 5. Contribution of each life phase to the total life cycle costs of the biogas plant compared to the environmental contribution of selected impact categories.

plant. Excluding climate change and water resources depletion, the con- tribution of this phase to total impacts ranges between 49.4% for photo- chemical ozone formation and 86.5% for ionizing radiation and freshwater eutrophication. Among the emission sources belonging to operation & maintenance phase, the most impacting ones are biogas combustion, purchased electricity and internal transport. These sources have crucial effects on ozone depletion, human toxicity, particulate mat- ter, ionizing radiation, photochemical ozone formation, acidification, freshwater eutrophication, freshwater ecotoxicity and CED.

Avoided impacts from the substitution of conventional treatment by anaerobic digestion have an important influence on environmental bal- ance of biogas system. In the case of climate change, these avoided im- pacts offset the GHG emissions of rest of life cycle phases studied in this work.

Utilization of the digestate from agri-food waste fraction as a fertil- izer improves the environmental performance of the biogas system, pri- marily in the human toxicity and freshwater ecotoxicity, due primarily to savings in the emission of metals in the EoL stage.

LCC analysis revealed that the life cycle phase with highest share of economic costs is the O&M phase, followed by the biogas plant (con- struction and dismantling). The results suggest that the relative eco- nomic benefits associated with the GHG emissions avoidance are much lower than their environmental relative repercussion. The NPV results indicated that the project is economically viable, since the IRR obtained (10.2%) is higher than the considered reasonable return on in- vestment (7.398%). The profitability of the project is primarily related to
Fig. 6. NPV value for the biogas plant assuming different discount rates.

the income obtained from the gate fees charged for the management of the biowastes. The contribution of electricity sales to the economics of the biogas system is significantly lower while the public subsidies ob- tained from the CLIMA Program have a negligible repercussion.
